survivors.finding: The pinned m12 claims-narrowing cannot type-check as specced — verified empirically with strict tsc. Two independent failures: (1) a 'type-only' override of an inherited accessor does not exist in TypeScript — both `declare readonly claims: ...` and class-interface declaration merging fail with TS2610 ('claims is defined as an accessor in class LumenizeClient, but is overridden here as an instance property'); the only compiling form is a getter re-declaration WITH a body (`get claims(): T { return super.claims as T }`), contradicting the spec's 'the runtime getter is unchanged'. (2) Even that getter fails TS2416: `Readonly<NebulaJwtPayload>` is not assignable to the base's `Readonly<JwtPayload> | null` because base JwtPayload (packages/auth/src/types.ts:69) requires `emailVerified: boolean`, which NebulaJwtPayload (packages/nebula-auth/src/types.ts:50) does not declare (it has `email`/`access` instead). The covariant narrowing the docs' strict-TS examples depend on is structurally impossible against the current type pair.
survivors.suggestion: Pin one of: (a) make LumenizeClient generic over the claims payload — `class LumenizeClient<TClaims extends object = JwtPayload>` with `get claims(): Readonly<TClaims> | null` — and have NebulaClient extend `LumenizeClient<NebulaJwtPayload>` plus re-declare the getter (with trivial `super.claims!` body) to drop the `| null`; (b) reconcile the payload interfaces (e.g. NebulaJwtPayload extends a shared base, or add the missing fields) so the narrowing is structural. Either way, reword 'type-only' in nebula-frontend.md:772 and api-reference.md:509 to 'behaviorally-neutral getter re-declaration' and note the mesh-side change if (a) is chosen.

survivors.location: nebula-frontend.md:709 ('Capture pre-write snapshots per (rt, rid) at transaction-submit time') and :892 ('captured at transaction-submit time ... the value local diverged from'); factory-conflict-outcome.md invariants 3 & 5; debounce-serial-queue.md D1 'txResultRollback ... pre-write value restored'
survivors.finding: The B4 fix's capture point contradicts the pinned optimistic-write model on the primary write path. Optimistic writes land in the store on EVERY keystroke (Decisions pinned :90; debounce-serial-queue.md:19), while transaction submission is debounced 500–2000 ms later. A 'pre-write snapshot' captured from the store AT TRANSACTION-SUBMIT TIME therefore already contains the user's edits — it equals `local`, not 'the value local diverged from'. Consequences: (a) `base === local`, which re-introduces the exact B4 degeneration through the back door (3-way merge collapses; concurrent server edits silently dropped for the flagship v-model/@longform path); (b) rollback on validation-failed/permission-denied/timeout 'restores' the unconfirmed optimistic value — a no-op revert. The wording is only correct for explicit `transaction()` calls where the store hasn't been pre-modified. The isolation detour will encode the wrong semantics into its property tests if built from this text.
survivors.suggestion: Redefine the capture point in all three files: the pre-write/base snapshot is taken when a resource's optimistic state FIRST diverges from last-confirmed — i.e., the synced-state middleware captures `oldValue` (full resource value) on the first local write of a debounce window — OR the factory maintains a last-confirmed snapshot per (rt,rid), updated on `committed`/`use-server`/remote write-through. 'At submit time' applies only to explicit transaction() calls with no pending middleware writes. Add a capable-of-failing test: type via the middleware path, conflict, assert base equals the pre-typing value (a submit-time-capture impl yields base===local and fails).

survivors.finding: The replay matrix specs unimplementable assertions: 'retry the identical ops with the same newETag across {create, put, delete, create+put} × {no churn, third-party churn ...}, asserting committed (NOT infrastructure-error/already exists)'. eTag-based replay detection (resources.ts Steps 4.5a/6.5) works only while at least one batch resource still carries `newETag`. When a third party churns EVERY written resource between original and retry, the evidence is gone by design (forward-only eTags, ADR-005, no dedupe ledger): a singleton put×churn or delete×churn replay returns a CONFLICT (Step 4.5b/9), and a singleton create×churn replay still throws 'already exists' at Step 7 — the very outcome the matrix forbids. Only the create+put×partial-churn cell is rescued by `.some`. As written, three of the eight cells cannot pass, and an implementer may 'fix' the tests by weakening the monotonic-pre-check discipline the B3 fix just established.
survivors.suggestion: Replace the blanket 'asserting committed' with per-cell expected outcomes: committed when ≥1 batch resource still carries newETag (no churn; partial churn); conflict (resolver fires, never 'already exists'/infrastructure-error... note create×full-churn currently DOES throw — needs its own pin, tied to the M11 create-on-existing taxonomy alternative) when all written resources were churned. Document full-churn replay-as-conflict as the known residual limit of eTag idempotency — it is also the precise thing the mesh-overload task's 'verify server replay-idempotency of newETag' ship-blocker must verify.

survivors.location: nebula-frontend.md:775 (pin 1) and :779 (pin 5); api-reference.md § client.orgTree / OrgTreeState (no coverage)
survivors.finding: The landed M8 namespace-enforcement pins leave two holes the original deep-review fix ('transaction path rejects creates on reserved rids (or key Subscribers by (rt, rid))') covered. (a) Pin 5(b) rejects only `create` with `typeName === 'lmz'` — nothing reserves the resourceId `orgTree` under OTHER types. Subscribers' PK is `(resourceId, clientId)` with no rt column (task :155-162), so an app resource ('todo','orgTree') shares rows with the framework tree subscription: a mutation to that app resource broadcasts its snapshot to every universal-tree subscriber WITHOUT a permission check (a cross-user value leak in the same class as B2, requiring only that some user created a resource with rid 'orgTree'), plus unsubscribe cross-kill and INSERT-OR-REPLACE clobber. (b) `transaction()` addressing the whitelisted ('lmz','orgTree') itself is unspecified — pin 1 only errors rids NOT on the allow-list, and pin 5(b) only covers `create`, so put/move/delete with typeName 'lmz' on rid 'orgTree' falls through to the normal Resources path and dies with an incidental 'not found' rather than a deliberate reserved-namespace rejection.
survivors.suggestion: Extend the pins: (i) `transaction()` rejects rt === 'lmz' for ALL rids — including whitelisted ones; the tree mutates only via client.orgTree.*; (ii) reserve the framework resourceId list across every type at create time (reject `create` whose resourceId is on the reserved list, any typeName), OR re-key Subscribers by (rt, rid) — the two alternatives deep-review M8 originally named. Add the security probe: create ('todo','orgTree') as user A, subscribe ('lmz','orgTree') as user B, mutate A's resource → assert B receives nothing.

survivors.location: § Lists with v-for, first-run bootstrap example, lines 302-313 (check at :310)
survivors.finding: The read-then-create bootstrap treats `outcome.kind !== 'ok'` as the only failure signal, but by the spec's own atomicity invariant (api-reference § TransactionOutcome), per-resource failures arrive UNDER `kind: 'ok'` — a create that resolves `permission-denied` or `validation-failed` yields `outcome.kind === 'ok'` with the failure in `outcome.resources[sub]`. The example then proceeds as if the list exists: the UI subscribes to a never-created resource (a server-side error) and renders a permanently blank list with no surfaced message. The founder demo can't hit it (admin on root), but the example explicitly generalizes ('In a multi-user app, attach under whatever node the user was granted'), so the Studio LLM will replicate the broken success check into exactly the flows where permission-denied is live. This is a new gap in how the M11 re-read fix landed — the re-read disambiguates infrastructure-error, not per-resource denial.
survivors.suggestion: Strengthen the success check: treat the create as landed only when `outcome.kind === 'ok' && outcome.resources[sub]?.kind === 'committed'`; otherwise re-read, and if still absent surface the per-resource failure (permission-denied → 'request access' guidance). One-line change to the example condition plus a sentence in the race comment.

survivors.finding: 'A per-call handler takes precedence over the per-type one for every resource in that call' reads as replace semantics — the exact misreading M9 fixed. It contradicts the same section's example comment 35 lines later ('layers in front of (does not replace) the per-type handlers', :397-401) and api-reference § Precedence. The Studio LLM consuming this sentence first could conclude a per-call handler suppresses sibling per-type handlers.
survivors.suggestion: Reword to match the pinned layering: 'A per-call handler is consulted first for every resource in the call; returning undefined falls through to the per-type handler (terminal reactions are additive) — see API reference § Precedence.'

survivors.finding: Invariant 3 lists `ontology-stale` inside the 'roll back ALL' parenthetical ('Rollback outcomes (timeout, infrastructure-error, ontology-stale → roll back ALL ...)') and then states the opposite in its own next sentence ('ontology-stale does NOT roll back (page reload expected)'). The master spec (nebula-frontend.md:696, api-reference.md:608) pins no-rollback. An implementer building the isolation tests from the parenthetical encodes the wrong behavior.
survivors.suggestion: Remove `ontology-stale` from the roll-back-ALL list in the parenthetical; keep the trailing no-rollback sentence.

survivors.finding: D2 still wires flush-on-blur via 'focus/blur event handlers attached by the v-model directive wrapper' — but the custom-directive design was retired by the same m3 fix that updated this file's pin (line 16: 'NOT a v-model.eager modifier (that design was retired)'). No directive-free blur mechanism is stated anywhere, yet flush-on-blur is a published contract (coding-your-ui.md:87, api-reference.md:371). The factory has no DOM hook of its own; without a stated mechanism the contract is unimplementable as specced.
survivors.suggestion: Replace the stale D2 bullet with a directive-free mechanism — simplest: a single capture-phase `focusout` listener registered by the factory at document level that flushes pending debounced writes (resource-level granularity makes 'flush everything pending on any blur' acceptable); or explicitly add 'pick the blur hook' to the D1 open-questions list.

survivors.location: transaction() Step 4.5b, lines 324-334 (pre-conflict fast-fail); delivered via star.ts:276-281 doTransaction
survivors.finding: The B3 conflict pre-check returns the full `currentSnapshot` (value + meta.changedBy.sub + meta.nodeId + eTag) to the caller BEFORE any permission check runs. An authenticated tenant user with NO read permission on resource R can submit `transaction({ R: { op:'put', eTag:'<any-wrong-uuid>', value:{} } })`; Step 1 reads R's snapshot with `#getCurrentSnapshot` (no permission gate, unlike the public `read()` at :248-254), Step 4.5b sees `current.meta.eTag !== op.eTag` and returns `{ ok:false, errors:{ R:{ type:'conflict', currentSnapshot:<full R> } } }`, and Star delivers it straight to the attacker's client where it lands as `server.value` in the store. This is a cross-user resource-read bypass of the DAG read-permission gate — the same class of hole as prior-round B2, but via a NEW path the B3 fix created. Pre-B3 the conflict was detected only inside transactionSync at Step 9, AFTER the Step 8 permission check, so an unauthorized caller received `permission-denied` and never the snapshot. The fix's monotonic-pre-check reasoning is correct about VERDICT correctness (replay/conflict are monotonic) but overlooked that the conflict pre-check DISCLOSES permission-gated data; note the idempotency pre-check (Step 4.5a) returns only `eTags` and leaks nothing, so the asymmetry is precisely the snapshot disclosure. This also makes resources.md:95's central promise ('Resource values are still gated by permission at every read and write') factually false in code.
survivors.suggestion: Gate the pre-conflict disclosure behind a permission check: before adding a resource to `preConflicts`, call `this.#dagTree.requirePermission(current.meta.nodeId, 'write')` and only disclose the snapshot on success (a denial there is non-monotonic but is a legitimate disclosure precondition — it will be re-decided authoritatively at Step 8). Simplest safe alternative: drop the Step 4.5b conflict pre-check entirely and keep only the Step 4.5a idempotency pre-check (which discloses no snapshot); conflicts then fall through to the in-txn Step 9 check, which already runs after the Step 8 permission gate. Either way, add the permission ordering to the Step 4.5 comment so the next reader sees that disclosure must follow permission.

survivors.location: 'permission-denied: non-admin user write resolves with permission-denied' (lines 165-191)
survivors.finding: The only permission-boundary test submits the unauthorized put with the CORRECT eTag (`createOutcome.eTag`, line 183), so the write never conflicts and the path runs into transactionSync where Step 8 returns permission-denied — it cannot catch the Step 4.5b pre-conflict disclosure. There is no capable-of-failing probe for 'unauthorized caller submits a put with a wrong/stale eTag must NOT receive the resource snapshot.' As written, the B3 disclosure regression ships green.
survivors.suggestion: Add a probe mirroring this test but with a deliberately wrong eTag: a non-permitted user submits `{ op:'put', eTag:'<random-uuid>' }` against a resource at a private node, then assert the outcome does NOT carry the resource value/meta (it must resolve as `permission-denied`, or at minimum must not return `currentSnapshot`). Gut the pre-check guard and the probe should fail (snapshot leaks).

survivors.location: describe 'authorization enforcement' → 'addEdge requires admin on the child' (L901-948)
survivors.finding: The B1 fix is a conjunction — addEdge requires write@newParent AND admin@child (dag-tree.ts:205-211) — but only the admin@child half has capable-of-failing coverage. In both probes Mallory holds admin on the parent (homeId), so admin⊇write satisfies the parent check implicitly. No test in the file exercises addEdge by an actor lacking write on the new parent (all other callStarAddEdge sites at L161/315/319/341/721/931/940 run as admins). Gut `this.requirePermission(parentNodeId, 'write')` from addEdge and the entire suite stays green — half of a security permission check is unverified. removeEdge's write@parent check (dag-tree.ts:233) is similarly uncovered.
survivors.suggestion: Add two probes: (a) user with admin on the child but zero grant on the prospective parent calls addEdge → expect 'write permission required'; (b) user with no grant on the parent calls removeEdge on an existing edge → denied. Both are five-line variants of the existing Mallory test.

survivors.finding: The matrix instruction — "retry the identical ops with the same newETag across {create, put, delete, create+put} × {no churn, third-party churn}, asserting committed (NOT infrastructure-error/already exists)" — asserts outcomes the (correct) implementation cannot deliver for 3 of 8 cells. Once a third party churns the SAME resource, its current eTag moves past newETag, making the replay undetectable (intrinsic to ADR-005's forward-only-eTag, no-dedupe-ledger design): put×churn-on-self and delete×churn-on-self replay resolve as `conflict` (resources.ts Step 4.5b), and create×churn-on-self still throws 'already exists' at Step 7 → infrastructure-error. Only the no-churn column and multi-resource partial-churn (the `.some` path, already shipped in nebula-client-read-transaction.test.ts:230-268) can assert committed. As written, a 5.3.8 implementer either writes tests that fail against correct code, weakens assertions to ship green (the exact testing.md anti-pattern), or 'fixes' the server with a dedupe ledger — an ADR-005 violation.
survivors.suggestion: Rewrite the matrix with per-cell expected outcomes: no-churn column → committed; churn defined as churn-on-a-batch-sibling → committed via .some; churn-on-the-replayed-resource-itself → put/delete expect conflict (document this as the ADR-005 boundary of 'retry-safe'), create expects the M11 create-on-existing outcome (tie this cell to the already-exists taxonomy decision flagged for Larry at v3 L773 — don't pin committed).

survivors.location: coding-your-ui.md L170-174 (disconnected banner) vs factory-conflict-outcome.md invariant 3 / api-reference.md L609 ('timeout' → roll back ALL) vs debounce-serial-queue.md flush-trigger list (L17) and D1 transitions (L60-67)
survivors.finding: The blessed banner says "Disconnected — your changes are queued. Reconnecting…" (comment: "Optimistic writes still queue in the background"), but the spec'd machinery contradicts it for any disconnect longer than the 5–10 s in-flight timeout: the conflict/outcome engine rolls back ALL optimistic writes on 'timeout', and for middleware-submitted debounced v-model writes there is no app await-site to retry from (the 5.3.4 'left as-is, app retries' pin predates framework-submitted writes; the Phase -1 #9 retryPolicy is explicitly deferred). Net: a >10 s blip silently reverts text the banner just promised was queued. No test obligation anywhere covers this seam: debounce-serial-queue.md has no disconnect flush/suspend trigger, zero timeout-path property assertions (the D0 harness supports 'timeout' outcomes but no probe uses them), and no InFlightWithBuffer × rollback/timeout transition (D1 only enumerates InFlight → Idle); factory-conflict-outcome's hold-pending-fanouts test covers a mid-edit fanout but not the reconnect re-subscribe initial-snapshot push clobbering pending optimistic state; 5.3.8's disconnect probe only checks re-subscribe delivery.
survivors.suggestion: Pin the disconnected-write contract before the detours build: most consistent with the banner is (a) suspend debounce flush + the in-flight timeout timer while lmz.connection is not 'connected', flush on reconnect (idempotent via newETag), OR (b) keep timeout-rollback and fix the banner text to be honest. Then add probes: D0 — flush fires while disconnected → no submission until reconnect, eTag chain intact; conflict-outcome — timeout/infrastructure-error while InFlightWithBuffer (what happens to buffered keystrokes); hold-pending-fanouts — reconnect initial-snapshot push arrives while a resource has pending optimistic state → held and merged, pre-disconnect keystrokes survive.

survivors.location: nebula-frontend.md §5.3.7-v3 Prerequisite line (~L676) and First-run container create item (~L773); coding-your-ui.md L297-316
survivors.finding: The flagship bootstrap (coding-your-ui L297-299: founder "holds admin on the root node (granted when the Star was created)") and the v3 two-tab race test both presuppose nebula-star-root-admin.md Part 1 (founder seeding) — which is spec'd, not started, and NOT in v3's prerequisite list (L676 names only the four detours). Two test-strategy consequences: (1) v3 can ship 'complete' with the first code path of every generated app broken for real founders; (2) the race test as worded will almost certainly run with scope-admin test users (claims.access.admin bypass, like every existing baseline admin client), which passes even if founder seeding is absent or broken — not capable of failing for the documented path (DAG admin grant on root, no JWT bypass). Additionally the cross-reference at L773 "see root-admin bootstrap below" dangles — no such section exists in nebula-frontend.md.
survivors.suggestion: Add nebula-star-root-admin.md Part 1 to the v3 prerequisite list (or an explicit v3 checklist dependency); fix L773's dangling reference to link tasks/nebula-star-root-admin.md; amend the race-test spec to require a seeded founder WITHOUT claims.access.admin (create-under-ROOT must succeed via the DAG grant alone, and fail if seeding is gutted).

survivors.location: vs resources.ts Step 4.5b (L324-334) and nebula-frontend.md 5.3.8 ~L972 (co-occurrence probe)
survivors.finding: The conflict+invalid-value co-occurrence probe (conflict wins, not validation-failed) is the ONLY test capable of failing if the landed Step-4.5b pre-check regresses — the shipped 'use-server' conflict test (L315-348) uses a valid value, so it passes whether the conflict is caught at 4.5b or at the post-validator Step 9. The code change is in the working tree NOW, but its one observable regression guard is scheduled in 5.3.8, a future phase. Same pattern for Step 6.5: in the partial-churn test the replay is caught at 4.5a, so 6.5 individually has no failing-mode coverage (acceptable as the optimization-layer framing, but worth a comment).
survivors.suggestion: Pull the co-occurrence probe into nebula-client-read-transaction.test.ts now: submit a put with a stale eTag AND an invalid value → expect resolution 'use-server'/conflict carrying currentSnapshot, not 'validation-failed'. ~15 lines using the existing two-client setup.

survivors.location: § Approach (L14-22) vs the 'Parity with property writes' test bullet (L30); api-reference.md § Middleware (L682-706)
survivors.finding: Option A's implementation note says the wrapper "runs the mutation and then triggers the same debounced submission" — but Option B was rejected precisely because it "fires *after* the mutation, so collection edits couldn't run the abort-capable middleware." Mutate-first makes abort prevent only the submission, not the local mutation — exactly the local-only divergence M10 exists to fix — and the abort-parity test would fail (or get weakened) against an implementation that follows the note literally. Separately, api-reference § Middleware never defines what a mutator-driven write delivers to middleware (path = the collection's path? oldValue/newValue = pre/post-mutation collection clones? what does throw-to-abort restore?), so the 'transform/abort sees collection mutations the same as property writes' assertion has no pinned expected shape to assert against.
survivors.suggestion: Reword the Approach: the wrapper computes the post-mutation value, runs the middleware chain BEFORE applying (abort ⇒ no local mutation, substitute ⇒ substituted value applied), then applies + triggers submission. Pin the middleware args for mutator calls in api-reference § Middleware (path of the owning collection, oldValue = pre-mutation snapshot, newValue = post-mutation value).

survivors.finding: Two edge cases the test list omits: (1) mutation during iteration — `for (const k of map.keys()) map.delete(k)` mixes a raw-bound iterator with the wrapped mutator; legal per Map semantics, but the wrapping + per-mutation submission path is exactly where it could misbehave (N submissions mid-iteration, or iterator invalidation through the proxy); (2) no-op mutators — `set.add(existingElement)`, `map.set(k, sameValue)`, `delete(absentKey)`, `clear()` on an empty collection. The set-trap path deep-equals-dedups identical writes; whether no-op mutators submit a transaction is unspecified and untested (a per-no-op transaction would be a silent write-cost leak given v-for-driven renders calling collection methods).
survivors.suggestion: Add two probes: delete-all-during-iteration coalesces to one submitted transaction and leaves the collection empty locally and on the mock; no-op mutators produce zero submissions (pin parity with the set-trap deep-equals skip in the file's 'One submission model' note).

survivors.location: § Property tests, 'Overlap is bounded' bullet (L21) vs 'Identity / degeneration' (L19) and 'Empty/edge' (L22)
survivors.finding: "never an empty string" contradicts the file's own identity rule and edge cases: local === server === '' ⇒ the result MUST be '' (identity rule: local===server ⇒ that value — e.g. both sides deleted all of base), and base='x', local='', server='' is the legitimate both-deleted-everything merge. An implementer writing the overlap assertion as stated produces a test that fails against a correct merge, or special-cases the merge to never return '' (corrupting the both-deleted case).
survivors.suggestion: Scope the never-empty assertion to overlap cases where at least one side's post-edit text is non-empty; keep 'never a crash' unconditional and add the both-sides-deleted-everything ⇒ '' case to the Empty/edge list explicitly.

survivors.location: §5.3.7-v3 connection-state replay item (~L768, no test bullet) + §5.3.8 connection-state probe (~L975); spike apps/nebula/spike/alpine-adapter/test/factory-basics.test.ts L326-345
survivors.finding: The replay-at-creation behavior that supersedes the old order-invariant has no capable-of-failing probe anywhere: the spike's connection tests and the 5.3.8 probe both create the factory first and then trigger transitions, so an implementation that wires only the transition observer (no creation-time capture) passes every enumerated test. The replay IS the documented contract (api-reference.md L57) and a regression target this round.
survivors.suggestion: Add to the v3 item (or 5.3.8) an explicit probe: drive the mock client to 'connected' BEFORE calling createNebulaClient, then assert store.lmz.connection.state === 'connected' / connected === true / lastConnectedAt set, with no transition fired after creation.

survivors.location: § Forms: explicit save, end of the handler-placement paragraph (L363): "A per-call handler takes precedence over the per-type one for every resource in that call."
survivors.finding: This closing sentence contradicts the pinned M9 semantics one paragraph before the example whose inline comment states them correctly ("layers in front of (does not replace)", L398-401) and api-reference § Precedence: for terminal branches the per-type handler STILL fires (per-call reactions are additive), and for 'conflict-pending' undefined falls through. "Takes precedence" is the exact pre-fix shadow mental model the Studio LLM must not pattern-match — a new defect in how the M9 wording-sync landed.
survivors.suggestion: Replace with: "A per-call handler layers in front of the per-type one for every resource in that call — undefined falls through on 'conflict-pending', and per-type terminal reactions still fire (see API reference § Precedence)."

survivors.location: L805 ('Star admin bypasses DAG checks'), L809 comment, L1085-1086 ('universe admin bypasses all DAG checks via Star admin flag'), L1105 comment
survivors.finding: dag-tree.ts:158 was corrected this round to "Galaxy/Universe-scope admin bypass (NOT a Star admin — that's a DAG `admin` grant on root)", and nebula-star-root-admin.md pins that 'Star admin' now means a DAG admin grant on ROOT_NODE_ID. But this same-round-modified test file still names the claims.access.admin bypass "Star admin" in four places — the next reader (or the flag-#5 reconciliation task) inherits the contradicting vocabulary from the tests that pin the behavior.
survivors.suggestion: Rename to 'scope admin (claims.access.admin) bypasses DAG checks' / 'universe admin bypasses all DAG checks via the scope-admin claim' and fix the two inline comments to match the dag-tree.ts:158 wording.

survivors.location: 'serial queue: concurrent transactions are applied in submit order' (L270-313)
survivors.finding: The test name (and the 'Fire three updates concurrently' comment) promises a concurrency probe, but the body await-chains each transaction (the inline comment at L288-291 admits it), so it passes with the serial queue entirely gutted — it actually proves sequential eTag chaining. The true concurrency property (buffered writes chain off the in-flight tx's resulting eTag) is owned by debounce-serial-queue.md D0, so this test just needs honest naming.
survivors.suggestion: Rename to 'sequential transactions chain eTags in order' and drop the 'concurrently' comments; optionally add a pointer comment that the concurrent-buffering property lands with D0/v3.

survivors.location: § Tests (use-this/retries-exhausted bullets) vs api-reference.md TransactionOptions L243 + OnTransactionResourceResolutionOptions L306
survivors.finding: maxRetries is pinned with two sources and a precedence (per-call > per-type registered > default 5), but every enumerated exhaustion probe (detour + 5.3.8) exercises only the default-5 path. The precedence is one off-by-one away from silently using the wrong cap.
survivors.suggestion: Add one table-test row to the detour: per-type registered with maxRetries 10, per-call maxRetries 3, always-conflicting use-this → 'retries-exhausted' with attempts: 3.

survivors.location: api-reference.md § Reserved state paths L474 ("store.lmz.* is framework-written only (user writes dropped)") vs v3 test bullets (L683-685, L784, L782 test f)
survivors.finding: Test obligations exist for meta.* writes (warned, pass through), ui/app pre-seed (m13a), and rt==='lmz' resource writes skipping middleware (orgTree test f) — but nothing covers user writes to the non-resource lmz paths (e.g. store.lmz.connection.connected = true): 'dropped' is asserted in the contract doc with no probe, and whether the drop is silent or debug-warned is unspecified.
survivors.suggestion: Add to the m13 test-gap fill: a user write to store.lmz.connection.* is dropped (path value unchanged) and emits a debug-sink warning (assert via setDebugSink per testing.md), and pin silent-vs-warned in api-reference § Reserved state paths.

survivors.location: § Lists with v-for, first-run bootstrap snippet (L302-313)
survivors.finding: The race-safe check `if (outcome.kind !== 'ok' && await read(...) === null) throw` has a blind spot the M11 fix introduced: under the pinned v3 contract, per-resource failures (`validation-failed`, `permission-denied`) arrive as `outcome.kind === 'ok'` with a non-committed entry in `outcome.resources` — so a create denied by permissions sails through both conditions and the app proceeds with no list, no error, and a forever-empty v-for (the same silent-blank-app class as M1/M5). The founder demo can't hit it (admin on root, trivially valid value), but this is the first-run pattern Studio's LLM replicates for multi-user apps where permission-denied is reachable (the snippet's own comment points non-founders at "whatever node the user was granted").
survivors.suggestion: Make the success condition explicit: `const created = outcome.kind === 'ok' && outcome.resources[sub]?.kind === 'committed';` then `if (!created && await client.resources.read('todoList', sub) === null) throw ...`. One extra line; closes the silent path and teaches the LLM that per-resource outcomes live under kind 'ok'.

survivors.location: § Text fields specifically — don't leave the default (L518)
survivors.finding: "The shipped `textMerge` helper uses a sensible default; you can swap in `diff-match-patch` or similar" invites the Studio LLM to add an external npm dependency — not possible in a generated Nebula app (deps are substrate-controlled: vue, @lumenize/nebula-frontend, DaisyUI) and contrary to the one-right-way posture. The build-vs-borrow question is an internal implementation decision (factory-textmerge.md owns it), not a consumer option. Same paragraph says "three-way merge (libdiff-based)" while api-reference.md L550 says "LCS-based" — terminology drift for the same function.
survivors.suggestion: Delete the "you can swap in diff-match-patch or similar" clause (custom merge = hand-written handler logic, already covered) and align "libdiff-based" to "LCS-based" to match api-reference § textMerge.

survivors.location: dag-tree.ts:245-277 (reparentNode); api-reference.md:421-423 + :439 ("Structural mutations (require write)"); resources.md:163 (addEdge rationale)
survivors.finding: The B1 fix hardened addEdge (write@newParent AND admin@child) on the explicit rationale that "adding a parent edge is an access grant in structural clothing — everyone with grants on/above the new parent gains cascaded access to the child's subtree" (dag-tree.ts:206-211, resources.md:163). reparentNode ALSO adds a parent edge — it deletes oldParent→child and inserts newParent→child (dag-tree.ts:266-273) — so it has the identical access-widening property: every user holding a grant on/above newParent gains cascaded access to child's subtree that they did not have before. But reparentNode only checks write@oldParent + write@newParent (dag-tree.ts:255-256); it never checks admin@child. A user with write on two sibling subtrees can graft a child node from one into a subtree where OTHER (third-party) collaborators on newParent gain admin-via-cascade on that child — the exact escalation B1 closed for addEdge, left open on reparentNode. The docs reinforce the gap: api-reference.md:439 lists reparentNode under "require write" with no admin@child caveat, and :423's comment says only "Requires write on BOTH the old and the new parent." This is the move-via-reparent variant of the self-service privilege-escalation the B1 finding addressed.
survivors.suggestion: Decide whether reparentNode should also require admin@child (consistent with addEdge — the structural-clothing argument applies identically) or whether the asymmetry is intentional (e.g. because reparent removes the old edge so the actor's own access is net-neutral). If kept asymmetric, add a one-line justification to api-reference.md:421-423 and resources.md explaining why moving an edge doesn't demand admin@child while adding one does; if hardened, add this.requirePermission(childNodeId, 'admin') to reparentNode and update the docs' permission table + a dag-tree.test.ts case.

survivors.location: nebula-frontend.md:972 (Phase 5.3.8 B3 matrix bullet); test file lines 230-268 ("partial-churn multi-resource replay")
survivors.finding: The task file §972 states the B3 replay matrix is "{create, put, delete, create+put} × {no churn, third-party churn}" and names "The create and create+put-with-churn cases are the regression guards — they throw against the pre-fix ordering," framed as "baseline coverage already shipped in nebula-client-read-transaction.test.ts." The shipped baseline actually covers two shapes: (1) single create, no churn (line 193); (2) create+CREATE with third-party churn on one (lines 237-240 are BOTH op:'create'). There is no create+PUT case, no delete-replay case, and no put-with-churn case in the file. The "create+put-with-churn" regression guard the task file claims is shipped does not exist; the actual second test is create+create-with-churn. The .some/.every distinction IS validated (by the two-create case), so the fix is covered — but the specific case-shape inventory in §972 mis-describes what shipped.
survivors.suggestion: Either correct §972 to say the shipped baseline covers {create-no-churn, create+create-with-churn} (and the rest of the matrix — put, delete, create+put — broadens in 5.3.8), or rename the second baseline test's intent to reflect it is create+create not create+put. Don't leave the task file asserting a regression guard that isn't in the tree.

survivors.finding: The §401 nuance lists the idempotent-no-op-before-permission cases as exactly three: "adding an edge that exists, removing one that doesn't, revoking an absent grant." The code has FIVE such short-circuit-before-permission paths: addEdge edge-exists (dag-tree.ts:203), removeEdge edge-absent (:231), revokePermission grant-absent (:366), deleteNode already-deleted (:285), and undeleteNode not-deleted (:302). The deleteNode/undeleteNode already-in-target-state cases also return success before requirePermission and are documented as "Idempotent" at api-reference.md:425/:428, but they're omitted from the §401 nuance's exhaustive-sounding list. This is a follow-on to prior finding n1 (which cited "existing edge, already-deleted node, absent grant" — already-deleted node WAS in n1's scope but didn't make the doc clause).
survivors.suggestion: Broaden the §401 parenthetical to cover all five — e.g. "adding an edge that exists, removing one that doesn't, revoking an absent grant, deleting an already-deleted node, or undeleting a live one — short-circuits to success before the permission check."

survivors.location: dag-tree.test.ts:901-948 ("addEdge requires admin on the child")
survivors.finding: The B1 test verifies the admin@child half of the new AND requirement (write@victim insufficient → admin@victim sufficient), with write@newParent satisfied throughout because Mallory holds admin (hence write) on her home node. It never independently exercises the write@newParent half — a caller holding admin@child but lacking write@newParent should still be rejected. That half is not a regression (addEdge always checked write@parent), but the test as written cannot distinguish "requires write@parent AND admin@child" from "requires admin@child only," so the documented AND is only half-covered by a capable-of-failing assertion. If reparentNode's admin@child gap (separate finding) is fixed by copying addEdge's pattern, this gap matters more.
survivors.suggestion: Add an assertion (or a sibling case) where the actor holds admin@child but no write on the new parent and confirm addEdge rejects with 'write permission required' — making the AND fully capable-of-failing on both conjuncts.

survivors.location: nebula-frontend.md:773 ("the loser's create currently throws 'already exists' → infrastructure-error")
survivors.finding: §773 says the M11 first-create race loser "currently throws 'already exists' → infrastructure-error, indistinguishable from an outage." The CURRENT shipped client has no infrastructure-error resolution — #mapSynchronousOutcome (nebula-client.ts:878-888) maps any thrown Error (other than OntologyStale) to { resolution: 'timeout' }. infrastructure-error is a new-in-v3 resolution (task §698). The prior M11 finding stated this precisely as "infrastructure-error (v3) / timeout (today)"; the §773 wording dropped the "(today=timeout)" qualifier when the fix landed, so the task file now mis-states the current code's behavior for the race loser.
survivors.suggestion: Restore the M11 precision in §773: "the loser's create currently throws 'already exists' which the shipped client surfaces as 'timeout' (v3: 'infrastructure-error') — indistinguishable from an outage either way."

survivors.finding: The Code Generation section still instructs: "Language model generates UI artifacts as plain HTML + Alpine-flavored x-* directives bound to @lumenize/state paths. This contradicts (a) the same file's § Studio-Generated Artifacts (line 38: UI = .vue SFCs per the 2026-05-15 pivot), (b) the same file's § Bootstrap files (createNebulaClient + Vue store), (c) coding-your-ui.md end-to-end (Vue SFC, store bindings), and (d) the pinned deletion of @lumenize/state/StateManager (nebula-frontend.md § Deletions). The bullet even cites the superseded 2026-05-09 pin without noting the 2026-05-15 reversal, while claiming its patterns are "documented in website/docs/nebula/coding-your-ui.md" — which now documents the opposite framework.

survivors.location: § Phase 5.3.7-v3 → "Nebula coding-your-ui.md doc unification" item, addTodo/saveTodo sketches (lines 841-877)
survivors.finding: The doc-unification item's inline sketches have drifted from what coding-your-ui.md actually ships: (1) addTodo reads `nodeId: list.nodeId` where `list = store.resources.todoList[client.claims.sub]?.value` — nodeId is not on value (TodoList = items + openCount only); the doc's final version (coding-your-ui.md:329-338) correctly uses `listSnap.meta.nodeId` and adds an `if (!list) return` guard; (2) the saveTodo sketch lacks the `if (delta !== 0 && !list) return` guard (deep-review m7) and the per-call onTransactionResourceResolution handler the final doc pins as the form pattern. The item also remains unchecked even though the doc work it describes is done, so a v3 implementer reading it as live instructions would regress the doc.

survivors.location: coding-your-ui.md § Lists with v-for, bootstrap snippet line 310; nebula-frontend.md line 773
survivors.finding: The race-safe first-create guard `if (outcome.kind !== 'ok' && await read(...) === null) throw` only catches transaction-wide failures. Under the pinned outcome taxonomy, per-resource failures (permission-denied, validation-failed) arrive under top-level `kind: 'ok'` with the failure in `outcome.resources[sub]` — so a user without write on the target node passes the guard silently, no list is created, no error surfaces, and the subsequent subscribe hits the auto-subscribe error path (path stays undefined → perpetual loading). The doc explicitly tells non-founder apps to reuse this pattern ("attach under whatever node the user was granted"). Both the doc and the task file (line 773) tell the same incomplete story, so this is a defect in how the M1/M11 fix landed, not a restatement of it.
survivors.suggestion: Extend the guard to also re-read when the create's per-resource resolution isn't 'committed': e.g. `const failed = outcome.kind !== 'ok' || outcome.resources[sub]?.kind !== 'committed'; if (failed && await read(...) === null) throw ...`. Mirror the same sentence in nebula-frontend.md line 773.

survivors.finding: The note quotes dag-tree.ts:158 as `// Star admin bypass` and asks whether the claims.access.admin bypass is "a higher Galaxy/universe-operator tier (keep, relabel) or a leftover (remove)". Leaving it framed as open invites relitigation of a pinned decision.

Note what `path`/`newValue` carry for a mutator-driven invocation, or mark that detail as pinned-during-D0.

survivors.location: § Lists with v-for — first-run bootstrap example, lines 302-313 (and the matching framing in tasks/nebula-frontend.md:773 'on a not-ok outcome re-read')
survivors.finding: The flagship read-then-create bootstrap guards only on the transaction-wide outcome: `if (outcome.kind !== 'ok' && await client.resources.read('todoList', sub) === null) throw ...`. But per the doc's own contract (api-reference § TransactionOutcome / TransactionResourceResolution), per-resource failures land INSIDE `kind: 'ok'` — a create that fails `permission-denied` (or `validation-failed`) returns `{ kind: 'ok', resources: { [sub]: { kind: 'permission-denied' } } }` (confirmed against resources.ts Step 8, which returns `{ ok: false, errors: { [rid]: { type: 'permission' } } }` → per-resource resolution, not a transaction-wide failure). The example's own comment invites reuse beyond the founder ('In a multi-user app, attach under whatever node the user was granted'), and for any user without write on the chosen node the code silently falls through with no list and no error — the subsequent todoList read in templates stays '...' forever. The re-read disambiguation (T4/M11 fix) only fires for the `infrastructure-error` race path; the per-resource failure path was left uncovered when the fix landed.
survivors.suggestion: Gate on per-resource success, not transaction-wide success: `const committed = outcome.kind === 'ok' && outcome.resources[sub]?.kind === 'committed';` then `if (!committed && await client.resources.read('todoList', sub) === null) throw new Error(...)`. Apply the same correction to the 'on a not-ok outcome re-read' wording in tasks/nebula-frontend.md:773 so the v3 two-client test also covers the permission-denied-inside-ok path.

survivors.location: § Forms: explicit save, line 363 (last sentence of the second paragraph)
survivors.finding: "A per-call handler takes precedence over the per-type one for every resource in that call." contradicts the pinned T7/M9 layering semantics stated everywhere else — api-reference § Precedence ("does **not replace** the per-type handler — it **layers in front of it**"; terminal branches: per-type fires for every resource, per-call is additive), resources.md:337, and the example's own inline comment 40 lines later ("layers in front of (does not replace) the per-type handlers"). "Takes precedence" reads as replacement — exactly the misreading M9 was fixed to prevent; an LLM pattern-matching this sentence would conclude the per-type handler is suppressed when a per-call handler is present.
survivors.suggestion: Replace with layering language: "A per-call handler is consulted first for every resource in that call; returning `undefined` falls through to the per-type handler (terminal reactions are additive — see API reference § Precedence)."

survivors.location: § Text fields specifically — don't leave the default, numbered steps 2-4 (lines 491-495)
survivors.finding: The race narrative says "Server fans out the new snapshot. The resource-update handler writes it to the store unconditionally." and "Their `v-model` input snaps to the other client's value mid-keystroke." This contradicts the pinned hold-pending-fanouts contract: api-reference § textMerge (line 556) states "the synced-state middleware holding fanouts while a resource has pending optimistic writes (shipped alongside this helper in v3)", and tasks/factory-conflict-outcome.md invariant 8 pins that incoming fanouts for a resource with pending optimistic state (debounced write outstanding OR transaction in flight) are HELD until the next submit's resolver merges. Under the v3 contract a mid-typing user never sees the mid-keystroke snap; the clobber risk is at conflict-resolution time (default 'use-server' rollback). The motivation for @longform still holds, but the doc teaches the Studio LLM the wrong timing model of the substrate.
survivors.suggestion: Rewrite steps 2-4 to the v3 contract: (2) another client commits; the fanout is held while this resource has pending optimistic writes; (3) the debounced submit conflicts (stale eTag) and the resolver sees both the full local state and the buffered server snapshot; (4) under the default 'use-server' the user's typing is rolled back to the server value at resolution time — which is why text-bearing types need @longform/textMerge.

survivors.location: § Internal mechanics → Middleware (lines 684-706) and § Reserved state paths set-trap note (line 487)
survivors.finding: "Fires on every write through the Proxy `set` trap" is now incomplete given the decided M10/T8 design: tasks/factory-collection-sync.md (Option A) pins that Map/Set mutator calls (`map.set/delete/clear`, `set.add/delete/clear`) are intercepted in the `get` trap and run "the **same** middleware chain, same submission, same ability for user middleware to transform/abort" (its tests assert middleware parity with property writes). A middleware author reading the published contract would conclude collection mutations bypass the chain. Additionally, the middleware payload for an intercepted mutator call (`path` = the collection's path? `oldValue`/`newValue` = pre/post-mutation collection?) is specified in neither artifact.
survivors.suggestion: Add a sentence to § Middleware (and align the § Reserved state paths set-trap note): writes include intercepted Map/Set mutator calls on collections under `store.resources.*.value`, which run the same middleware chain as property assignments. Decide and document the payload shape middleware receives for a mutator call (suggested: path = the collection path, oldValue/newValue = pre/post-mutation collection snapshots).

dropped.location: coding-your-ui.md:302 (read-then-create keyed on `=== null`); resources.ts:194-197 (`put` preserves `deleted`)
dropped.finding: The first-run bootstrap and the tombstone contract corner each other: `read()` returns non-null for a soft-deleted todoList (tombstone with last value), so the bootstrap skips create; `create` on the existing (deleted) row would throw 'already exists' anyway; and `put` preserves `deleted: true` (resources.ts:197), so no client surface can ever resurrect a deleted resource — resources have no undelete op (unlike orgTree nodes). A user whose container was ever deleted is permanently stuck rendering a tombstone, and the docs nowhere state that resource deletion is irreversible from the client.

With the Option A decision (factory-collection-sync.md), Map/Set mutator calls run the same middleware chain as property writes ('indistinguishable from property writes', incl. user-middleware abort/transform). But § Middleware still says it 'fires on every write through the Proxy set trap' and never mentions collection mutators — and the `path`/`oldValue`/`newValue` shape for a `map.set('k', v)` is defined nowhere. A user-middleware author (or the Studio LLM) cannot derive the new-in-v3 behavior from the contract doc.
